---
title: "Unit 5 — Approximate Bayesian Inference"
subtitle: "Four ways to one posterior: graphical model · MAP (Newton) · Laplace · MCMC"
engine: markdown
---

## Overview

This unit recovers **one** Bayesian posterior **four ways** and compares them. The model is
logistic regression with a Gaussian prior on the weights, fit to a small two-feature
binary-classification dataset. You'll draw its **graphical model**, find the **MAP** by
Newton–Raphson, build the **Laplace** Gaussian approximation at the mode, and sample the
posterior with **Metropolis–Hastings MCMC**. The payoff is the comparison — same posterior,
four costs and accuracies — and one lesson that matters: the posterior **mode is not the
mean**.

## Learning objectives

By the end of Unit 5 you will be able to:

- **Draw the PGM** — nodes, edges, and plate, with $\sigma^2$ as a random-variable node.
  *(Analyze)*
- **Find the MAP** by Newton–Raphson. *(Apply)*
- **Build the Laplace covariance** $(-\mathbf H)^{-1}$ at the mode. *(Apply)*
- **Implement MH-MCMC** and recover the posterior. *(Apply/Analyze)*
- **Derive** the Newton–Raphson (Poisson) and Laplace (Beta–Binomial) update rules. *(Analyze)*
- **Verify the four methods agree**; compare cost and accuracy; explain mode vs mean.
  *(Evaluate)*

## What you'll do

Recover one posterior five ways:

- **Way 1 — graphical model** *(structure)* — draw the PGM and state the conditional
  independence it encodes.
- **Ways 2–4 — MAP, Laplace, MCMC** *(code)* — implement the MAP (Newton–Raphson), the Laplace
  covariance $(-\mathbf H)^{-1}$, and a seeded Metropolis–Hastings sampler, then check they
  agree (the MAP mode sits inside the MCMC bulk; Laplace matches the MCMC spread).
- **Way 5 — derive the methods** *(derivation)* — the Newton update for a Poisson likelihood
  and the Laplace approximation for a Beta–Binomial.

Then answer three interpretation questions. **Any correct method passes** — tests are recovery,
properties, and cross-method agreement, never pinned MCMC draws — so **seed your sampler**.

## Prerequisites & connections

**Review first.** The matrix-calculus identities and Hessian thinking from Unit 2b; the
Gaussian prior and conjugacy ideas from Unit 4; gradients and optimization; seeded sampling
(Unit 0).

**Connections.**

> This is where the threads meet: the **Hessian uses Unit 2b's identity A3**, the prior on
> $\sigma^2$ comes from **Unit 4**, and the MCMC sampling extends **Unit 3's** estimate-by-drawing
> idea. The MAP → Laplace → MCMC progression is the spine of approximate inference and the
> toolkit you'll draw on in the **Capstone**.

## How it's graded

Three bundles, specifications not points. This unit's **Correctness** (core/gateway) is the
largest in the course and combines four kinds of check: **autograded** (MAP, Laplace, MCMC, and
cross-method agreement), **human structural** (the graphical model), and **human per-step
derivation** (the two update rules). **Interpretation** scores your CERL answers (Claim,
Evidence, Reasoning, Limits) and **Process** covers seeding, a cheap import, and a clean
render. Any unmet bundle gets one **revise-and-resubmit**; autograded resubmissions are
unlimited until the deadline. Full policy in [`GRADING.md`](../GRADING.md).

## What to submit

- Your completed **`hw.py`** — `logistic`, `gradient`, `hessian`, `newton_map`,
  `laplace_covariance`, `MetropolisSampler` (signatures unchanged; sampler seeded).
- Your **graphical model** and the conditional independence it encodes.
- Your **derivation write-ups** — the Poisson Newton–Raphson update (D1) and the Beta–Binomial
  Laplace approximation (D2).
- Your **rendered notebook** with the four-ways figure and interpretation answers (I1–I3).

*Submit via the course page — [submit mechanism TBD].*

## Deadline & revision window

- Due: **[PLACEHOLDER]**
- Post-feedback revision window: **[PLACEHOLDER]**

## Estimated effort

Roughly **8–11 hours [PROPOSED]** — the largest core unit: a diagram, three estimators that
must agree, and two derivations, plus the cross-method comparison.
